source.title: Introduce RAG cold-start warmup quickstart with edge prefetch and wasm preprocessor
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: cold-start latency is a common pain for edge-deployed RAG microservices where first requests trigger model warming and index priming; this quickstart demonstrates an opinionated pattern (edge prefetch + lightweight WASM request preprocessor + adaptive prefix cache) to reduce first-request tail latency without changing server-side runtime. What this PR adds: a new quickstart 'rag-coldstart-warmup' with README, a small TypeScript edge worker example that prefetches likely context, a WASM preprocessor Dockerfile and build scaffold, sample Terraform module to deploy to an edge Lambda/edge VM, a small indexed sample corpus and manifest.yaml, and a GH Actions smoke job that validates end-to-end cold-start latency under controlled conditions. Checklist: [x] Quickstart README with architecture diagram and step-by-step run instructions; [x] Local dev script (scripts/run-local.sh) that runs the quickstart in Docker with sample index; [x] Example prefetch policy (heuristics.json) and adaptive prefix cache config; [x] WASM preprocessor build + simple JS shim demonstrating header-based routing and request normalization; [x] Smoke CI job to verify startup and a simple query baseline. Usage summary: follow README to run locally: cd quickstarts/rag-coldstart-warmup && yarn && make build && ./scripts/run-local.sh --index small; the quickstart launches a light HTTP shim (edge prefetcher), a local mini-indexer, and a Redwood dev-runner configured to use a pinned small model variant; sample queries and expected latency numbers are in README. Implementation notes: the edge worker demonstrates speculative fetch of up to three candidate passages using low-cost metadata heuristics before the first token is requested; the WASM preprocessor normalizes incoming tool-call payloads and enforces a size cap to avoid oversized first requests; the adaptive prefix cache is configured to hold the top-K prefix KV entries per session for 30s by default which reduces warm KV misses for repeated short-lived sessions. CI/QA: GH Actions smoke job 'rag-warmup-smoke' executes a cold-start timeline measurement and fails if 95th percentile first-token latency exceeds 1.2x baseline; unit tests cover the preprocessor and heuristic policy; integration test runs a headless scenario with synthetic traffic. Backwards compatibility: purely opt-in quickstart; server side expects no protocol changes. Related items: reference design doc in DOCS-198 and ENG-3456 for runtime knobs and perf targets; follows patterns in existing quickstarts but emphasizes an edge-first prefetch strategy and a WASM-based preprocessing shim. Migration notes: none for runtime, this is training material and sample configs only. Merge summary and outcome: 6 commits squashed into one merge, all CI green after a single re-run of flaky e2e, merged via squash. Links: internal linear ENG-3456 (https://linear.app/company/ENG-3456) and DOCS-198 (https://linear.app/company/DOCS-198).
source.commits: Add quickstart scaffold and README; Add edge prefetch worker example and wasm preprocessor Dockerfile; Add sample index and manifest; Add scripts/run-local.sh and smoke CI job; Tweak docs and tidy scripts; Fix linting after reviewer feedback
review_comments: Diego Alvarez: Nice pattern, can we make the prefetch heuristic pluggable? Please add config hooks for custom heuristics.
Maya Chen: Added a heuristics.json and documented extension points in README; kept a simple default for the quickstart.
Priya Natarajan: Tests look good but the WASM build step is heavy for CI; can we cache the toolchain?
Maya Chen: Added a GH Actions cache step and a lightweight wasm-opt fallback for the smoke job to keep CI fast.
Samir Patel: One nit: clarify expected cost tradeoffs in the README and call out when this is inappropriate (high churn workloads).
Maya Chen: Expanded README to include cost/benefit section and sample telemetry dashboards to monitor prefetch hit rate.
source.release_notes: Add RAG cold-start warmup quickstart demonstrating edge prefetch + WASM preprocessor + adaptive prefix cache; includes sample configs, Terraform edge module, and a smoke CI job for cold-start latency validation.
source.notes_on_testing: Local validation: run scripts/run-local.sh --index small then run ./scripts/bench-first-token.sh to measure cold-start; CI runs rag-warmup-smoke.yml which measures 95th percentile first-token latency and basic functional assertions.
source.estimate_runtime: Under 15 minutes to run the quickstart locally on a developer machine with Docker installed.

source.title: introduce-unified-rag-starterkit-multilang-bindings-and-docsite-snippets
source.author: Nora Kim
source.release_notes: Adds a compact, opinionated RAG starter kit with ready-to-run TypeScript, Python, and Go examples; includes telemetry adapter shims and docs snippets for quick authoring.
source.description:
Motivation: Customers repeatedly asked for a compact, multi-language RAG starter that is small enough to fork as a template but comprehensive enough to cover model selection, embeddings, caching, and observability. This PR introduces a unified starter kit that: 1) provides TypeScript, Python, and Go reference runners; 2) includes small telemetry adapter shims that wire into Redwood Console probes; 3) adds docsite snippets and quickstart exercises so users can get from zero to a running RAG demo in under 10 minutes.

What changed (high level): - new starter directory with language-specific runners and minimal infra (docker-compose + local index) - telemetry adapters that emit the same semantic events we use in the Console (session_start, retrieval_hit, model_call, latency_bucket) - compact README and step-by-step Quickstart docs added to the docs snippets directory - small helper library for typed adapter bindings (TS/Python/Go) to demonstrate cross-language contract patterns - CI smoke tests and a local e2e script to exercise streaming and embeddings.

Checklist: - [x] Add TS/Python/Go runner templates - [x] Implement telemetry shim and examples - [x] Add docsite snippet pages and quickstart guide - [x] Add CI smoke scenarios that run locally in GitHub Actions - [x] Add CHANGELOG entry and release notes draft - [x] Validate minimal resource configuration for local dev (docker-compose)

Testing notes: The branch contains a lightweight GH Action that runs the smoke scenario (index build + single query per runner). Initially the Python runner failed intermittently due to a race in the local index seeder; added a short retry loop and re-ran CI. All checks are passing at merge. Manual verification steps are listed in the Quickstart README and were validated on macOS and Linux in a dev VM.

Backward compatibility: No API surface changes to Redwood platforms. Everything here is sample code and docs; no production runtime changes. The typed adapter helpers are opt-in and live in the examples namespace.

Related: ENG-2142 (starter kit initiative), PM-1098 (quickstart conversion for docs site), SUP-4021 (customer ask from onboarding).

feat: telemetry adapter shim and event schema examples
docs: add quickstart snippets and step exercises for docsite

ci: add smoke e2e workflow and matrix for languages

starter/unified-rag/telemetry/adapter-shim.ts: exported emitEvent(eventName, payload) and mapping to Console probe names
review_comments: Ethan Park: Nice compact layout. A few nit comments on naming and one suggestion to move the adapter event names to a shared constants file so examples don't drift.
Nora Kim: Thanks — extracted event names into telemetry/events.ts and updated bindings across languages.
Marisol Vega: Confirmed the quickstart steps work on my Linux laptop. Suggest adding a short troubleshooting section for common Docker networking issues.
Nora Kim: Added troubleshooting notes and an FAQ section to quickstart README.
Jonah Reed: CI smoke looks good. One test is timing-sensitive; suggest increasing timeout in the e2e script to reduce flakes.
Nora Kim: Increased timeout and added limited retry; reran CI — green.
source.ci_log_summary: Initial run: 1 failing job (py-runner smoke) due to index seeder race. Fix applied and rerun. Final: all checks passed (unit checks: pass, lint: pass, smoke-e2e: pass).
source.notes_for_release: Include Quickstart under 'Getting Started' -> 'Examples' section. Point docsite snippet to the starter kit repo path. Recommend highlighting telemetry interop as an end-to-end example in the March docs update.
post_merge_tasks: Add docsite snippet to promotion pipeline
Create small tutorial video for the starter kit (marketing request)
Monitor GH issues for onboarding questions for 2 weeks
source.observability_impact: Adds example telemetry events only; no production metrics changes. ADDED: examples of session_start, retrieval_hit, model_call, token_latency_bucket to Console.
source.how_to_try_locally: git clone <repo> && cd starter/unified-rag && docker-compose up --build; follow starter/README.md (TS/Python/Go subfolders include README steps). Use scripts/e2e_local_run.sh to validate all runners.
source.author_notes: This starter is intentionally opinionated: it shows one recommended retrieval + embedding + caching pattern and how to wire telemetry. Feedback welcome — we can expand alternative variants in follow-up PRs.

source.title: introduce-modular-rag-studio-lite-quickstart
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Adds a compact, modular RAG Studio quickstart aimed at developer demos and offline authoring. Motivation: we frequently field requests for a lightweight, dependency-minimal sandbox that demonstrates tool-calling, incremental indexing, and scenario-driven evaluations without needing heavy infra. This quickstart targets two personas: 1) engineers who want a reproducible local demo for product meetings and 2) contributors who need an isolated harness for writing scenario-based evals. Change summary: 1) new CLI scaffolder (bin/mini-rag) that bootstraps a tiny web UI, an in-memory incremental indexer, and a WASM-based tool stub runtime; 2) compact devcontainer and Docker profile for one-command local runs; 3) a small set of authored scenarios (samples/) with step assertions and a recorder that captures model/tool interactions for playback; 4) lightweight telemetry stub that emits structured events to a local file for debugging; 5) documentation pages (docs/quickstarts/mini-rag-studio.md) and a short HowTo for embedding the quickstart into demos. Backwards compatibility: the quickstart is entirely self-contained under quickstarts/mini-rag-studio and does not change global CLI commands. Security: no remote telemetry by default; all network calls are disabled in dev mode except optional feature flags. Runbook + checklist included in the description. See commits and changed files for details.
commits: Initial scaffold: add mini-rag CLI, basic web UI skeleton, and package.json
Add in-memory incremental indexer and sample indexer adapter
Implement WASM tool shim and example tool scripts
Add scenario recorder, playback runner, and sample scenarios with assertions
Add devcontainer, Docker profile, and a compact README for one-command launch
Docs: add quickstarts/mini-rag-studio.md and usage guide
Fix lint, add CI smoke test to run quickstart in headless mode
Address review feedback: tighten telemetry, add feature flag for network, update error messages
review_comments: Luca Martins: Nice minimal surface — can we ensure the devcontainer avoids mounting host creds by default? (request change)
Ava Chen (author): Good call — devcontainer now mounts only the repo root and includes a disabled secrets mount; documented in README (reply)
Priya Singh: The WASM stub is clever; add a comment on why we choose WASM over a sandboxed Python script to make the design choice explicit (request change)
Ava Chen (author): Added design rationale block in docs/quickstarts/mini-rag-studio.md and inline comments in tools/wasm_stub (reply)
Diego Alvarez: CI smoke is flaky locally for Windows users; can we fallback to a simpler invocation when headless browser fails? (comment)
Ava Chen (author): Added headless fallback and animation timeout guard; updated quickstarts-minirag-ci to tolerate transient timeouts (reply)
Luca Martins: Approved after fixes (approved)
source.release_notes: Introduce ‘mini RAG Studio’ quickstart: lightweight local sandbox demonstrating incremental indexing, WASM-based tool stubs, scenario recorder/playback, and a one-command devcontainer run. Intended for demos, contributor onboarding, and writing scenario-based evals.

source.title: Add introspective RAG playground + debugger walkthrough and probe adapters
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Add a new developer quickstart: 'RAG Introspection Playground' — includes guided walkthrough, token-level traces, retrieval trace visualizer, TS/Python runners, and a lightweight telemetry probe for local experimentation.
source.description:
Context and motivation:
This quickstart helps engineers debug RAG pipelines end-to-end in a reproducible local environment. Teams building retrieval-augmented features have asked for better visibility into where failures and hallucinations originate (retriever, index, ranking, or model generation). The aim of this PR is to provide a small, opinionated playground that surfaces retrieval traces, prefix/KV cache snapshots, and token-level model call traces — while keeping everything runnable with a single devcontainer + smoke CI job.

What this PR adds (high-level):
- A new quickstart workspace 'rag-introspection' with README-guided walkthrough and sample corpora.
- Devcontainer configuration for instant onboarding and preinstalled tooling (Python, Node, yarn, poetry).
- Two minimal runners: a TypeScript 'playground-runner' and a Python 'play-run' that demonstrate instrumented calls to the Redwood API and a stubbed local retriever.
- Retrieval trace visualizer: a tiny static HTML/JS viewer that shows retrieval candidates, scoring, and span highlights (no external services required).
- Token-level generation trace adaptor: middleware that records per-token timing, KV-cache hits, and emitted logits (sanitized for privacy).
- Lightweight telemetry probe (YAML) and an e2e smoke test that validates the tracing pipeline.
- Docs site update linking the new quickstart under /docs/quickstarts/rag-introspection and a compact troubleshooting section.

Why this approach:
- Focused visibility: we purposely keep the example small and instrumented so teams can copy the adapters into their Dedicated or Private deployments.
- Non-invasive: runtime adapters are designed as shims that sit between Redwood SDK calls and the model client — they can be toggled in production.
- Reproducibility: devcontainer + smoke CI ensures the quickstart works cross-platform and is maintained by CI.

Checklist (completed):
- [x] Add quickstart scaffold and README walkthrough
- [x] Implement TS and Python runners with instrumentation hooks
- [x] Add retrieval trace visualizer and sample index
- [x] Add telemetry probe + smoke CI job
- [x] Update docs site with walk-through and troubleshooting
- [x] Add unit/integration smoke tests and CI guard

Testing performed:
- Local devcontainer run: executed the full walkthrough (indexing sample docs, running TS runner, verifying trace output).
- CI smoke job: runs in GitHub Actions against a mocked Redwood API endpoint; traces and telemetry are validated.
- Manual cross-check: ensured the adapters do not leak raw documents or PII; all traces are redacted by default.

Related links and references:
- Linear: ENG-4212 (RAG visibility tools), PM-88 (quickstart priority)
- Docs target: /docs/quickstarts/rag-introspection (new page)
- Example runners: examples/rag-introspection/ts-runner and examples/rag-introspection/python-runner

Notes for reviewers:
- The core instrumentation is intentionally small and well-documented in the README — please verify the privacy/redaction section.
- The retrieval visualizer is a static artifact (no backend) to make it safe for demos.
- If you want to test with a live model, set the environment variable PLAYGROUND_USE_REMOTE_MODEL=true; otherwise the runners use the built-in mock model for deterministic traces.

Commits (summary):
- feat(rag-introspection): add scaffold, README, and sample corpus
- feat(runner): add TypeScript runner with tracing middleware
- feat(runner): add Python runner and example notebook
- feat(visualizer): add retrieval trace visualizer (static HTML/JS)
- feat(telemetry): add probe yaml and CI smoke job
- docs: add quickstart page and troubleshooting section

Merge notes:
- CI passed after one re-run of the smoke job (flaky network on first run).
- Squash-merged to main after approvals.

feat(runner): add Python runner and example notebook

Maya Chen (author): Added 'redaction levels' section and a default redaction policy in the telemetry probe config.
Lucia Gomez: The retrieval visualizer is great; suggest adding keyboard navigation for long lists in a follow-up. Approving once CI is green.
Ethan Park: Minor nit: prefer 'play-run' name for the Python runner to match other quickstarts. Author agreed and renamed the binary in the last commit.

text:
Context and motivation:
This quickstart helps engineers debug RAG pipelines end-to-end in a reproducible local environment. Teams building retrieval-augmented features have asked for better visibility into where failures and hallucinations originate (retriever, index, ranking, or model generation). The aim of this PR is to provide a small, opinionated playground that surfaces retrieval traces, prefix/KV cache snapshots, and token-level model call traces — while keeping everything runnable with a single devcontainer + smoke CI job.

What this PR adds (high-level):
- A new quickstart workspace 'rag-introspection' with README-guided walkthrough and sample corpora.
- Devcontainer configuration for instant onboarding and preinstalled tooling (Python, Node, yarn, poetry).
- Two minimal runners: a TypeScript 'playground-runner' and a Python 'play-run' that demonstrate instrumented calls to the Redwood API and a stubbed local retriever.
- Retrieval trace visualizer: a tiny static HTML/JS viewer that shows retrieval candidates, scoring, and span highlights (no external services required).
- Token-level generation trace adaptor: middleware that records per-token timing, KV-cache hits, and emitted logits (sanitized for privacy).
- Lightweight telemetry probe (YAML) and an e2e smoke test that validates the tracing pipeline.
- Docs site update linking the new quickstart under /docs/quickstarts/rag-introspection and a compact troubleshooting section.

Why this approach:
- Focused visibility: we purposely keep the example small and instrumented so teams can copy the adapters into their Dedicated or Private deployments.
- Non-invasive: runtime adapters are designed as shims that sit between Redwood SDK calls and the model client — they can be toggled in production.
- Reproducibility: devcontainer + smoke CI ensures the quickstart works cross-platform and is maintained by CI.

source.title: Introduce snapshot-branch authoring quickstart for iterative RAG scenario branching
source.author: Priya Shah
source.description:
Problem: teams authoring RAG scenarios frequently need to iterate variations of prompts, tool bindings, and small index changes in isolation and then merge the best variants back into a canonical scenario. Existing quickstarts show single-file examples or full-playground runners but lack a lightweight, reproducible branching workflow for local authoring and telemetry-driven selection.

What this PR adds: a Snapshot-Branch Quickstart that provides a simple authoring loop: capture a snapshot of the current scenario, create a branch, run iterative edits (prompts, tool contracts, index seed), record deterministic telemetry for each run, and merge a chosen snapshot back into the main scenario. The quickstart is intentionally minimal: no server required, runs locally with the Redwood CLI and the quickstart runner, and includes a devcontainer for reproducible environments.

Key features: snapshot and branch metadata format (YAML + JSON), a tiny runner that replays scenarios deterministically (samples seeded), stepwise telemetry presets for comparing outputs, an example branching workflow (create/compare/merge), devcontainer + smoke CI for reproducibility, and docsite pages with guidance on evaluation criteria.

Usage summary: 1) git clone; 2) open devcontainer or run ./scripts/bootstrap.sh; 3) redwood quickstart run --snapshot create --name=baseline; 4) edit quickstarts/snap-branch/scenario.yaml to try a prompt variant; 5) redwood quickstart run --snapshot branch --from=baseline --name=variant-A; 6) run ./scripts/compare_snapshots.py --left=baseline --right=variant-A to produce a concise diff and telemetry summary; 7) merge with redwood quickstart snapshot merge --source=variant-A --target=baseline if chosen.

Compatibility: works with Redwood CLI (v1.8+), uses the local-hosted test runner (no cloud account required). The quickstart is designed to be safe for enterprise (no secret material stored); optional telemetry uploads are gated behind explicit consent.

Testing and CI: included small e2e smoke test that runs two snapshot branches and asserts stable determinism. CI workflow runs on push and verifies devcontainer build + smoke e2e.

Acceptance criteria: documentation page added, quickstart runner script with tests, devcontainer recipe, telemetry presets file, and a compact example scenario demonstrating branching.

Related: ENG-2104 (authoring UX), DOC-472 (quickstart doc site slot).

chore: update package.json scripts and README examples
docs: add diagram and merge flow svg
review_comments: Ethan Park: Nice narrow surface area. Can we make sure the compare script handles tokenization differences across engines? Suggested adding a token-normalize flag.
Priya Shah (author): Good call — added a --normalize-tokens flag to compare_snapshots.py and documented engine compatibility in rag-authoring.md.
Lina Gomez: Telemetry looks fine but ensure no PII is collected by default. Add explicit opt-in and a README note about GDPR considerations.
Priya Shah (author): Telemetry uploads are now off by default; dev-mode preserves local files only and the README includes a privacy section.
John Wang: Devcontainer image is slightly heavy. Could we switch to the smaller node:18-slim base to make the startup faster?
Priya Shah (author): Switched to node:18-slim and trimmed unnecessary apt packages; image size reduced ~180MB.
Ethan Park: CI flake observed in e2e on first run due to RNG seed missing in one test. Please ensure seed is always set in runner.
Priya Shah (author): Fixed — seed propagated to all runner invocations and added a guard in tests to fail on missing seed.
Lina Gomez: Approving pending the small README grammar nit (line 42).
Priya Shah (author): Fixed the grammar nit.
John Wang: Approved.

source.title: Introduce stepwise RAG authoring kiosk and Go agent examples with telemetry probes
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: This PR adds a compact stepwise authoring "kiosk" quickstart for building RAG workflows and a minimal Go-based example agent that demonstrates tool-calling, structured output, and telemetry probes. Motivation: provide a low-friction, reproducible onboarding path for engineers who want to prototype multi-step retrieval workflows locally and validate them with lightweight step assertions. The kiosk is intentionally opinionated: it exposes a guided step sequencer, live index preview, and a watch-mode that mirrors edits to a local authoring file. The Go sample agent demonstrates secure tool invocation patterns, streaming handling, and a small telemetry shim that emits step-level events compatible with Redwood Console. Checklist: - [x] Add stepwise authoring UI quickstart with README, run instructions, and configuration templates - [x] Add Go sample agent with tool stubs and example tests - [x] Add telemetry probe YAML schema and example probe rules - [x] Integrate smoke CI job for quickstart validation - [x] Provide migration notes and compatibility guidance (no breaking API changes) How to run locally: 1) git clone the repo and cd quickstarts/rag-kiosk 2) docker-compose up --build to start the kiosk (or run the dev script for watch mode) 3) in another shell, cd golang/sample-agent && go run ./cmd/agent to start the example agent 4) follow the README-guided scenario to step through a retrieval + tool call sequence Notes on telemetry: the probes/ directory contains small example YAML rules that assert presence/ordering of events (step.enter, step.exit, tool.call). These are intentionally pluggable; customers can adapt them to Redwood Console ingestion. CI and test strategy: added a lightweight smoke job runs the kiosk in headless mode and exercises the Go agent over localhost; tests are intentionally small to keep CI latency low. Rollout and compatibility: this is an additive quickstart; it does not modify existing APIs or infra. Recommended canary: ship as preview docs to docs.redwood.ai and monitor telemetry event rates for the sample scenario. See release_notes for user-facing guidance.

a7d4e19: add Go sample agent with tool stubs and simple e2e test

d2a7b70: add CI smoke job, update docs and troubleshooting section

source.title: Add RAG Orchestration Smokehouse walkthrough and reproducible smoke CI
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a compact RAG orchestration walkthrough (Smokehouse) plus a deterministic smoke CI that validates tool-calling and stepwise rollouts against a seeded local index.
source.description:
Motivation: provide a compact, reproducible authoring + test walkthrough for building RAG-based orchestrations that call external tools. Many teams ask for a single, batteries-included example that covers: index seeding, simple tool-binding (HTTP webhook + local mock), stepwise agent execution, and a smoke CI that can run locally or in CI to catch regressions to tool-calling and structured output.

What this PR adds (summary):
- A lightweight ‘smokehouse’ quickstart under quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/ that includes: README, seed index script, tiny in-process tool-mock server, example orchestration YAML, and an idempotent smoke CI runner.
- Two example agent patterns: stepwise-orchestrator and event-driven-saga (small, focused code + config in TypeScript).
- A reproducible smoke CI harness (node-based) that runs against a pinned dev model variant and asserts structured outputs for three core scenarios (faq lookup, invoice-tool-call, and multi-step refinement).

Commits (high-level):
- feat: add smokehouse directory with README and starter templates (Ava Chen)
- feat: implement local tool-mock server and CI runner (Ava Chen)
- docs: walkthrough with troubleshooting, telemetry knobs, and rollout guidance (Priya Nair)
- test: add deterministic fixtures + e2e smoke assertions (Diego Morales)

Files changed (high-level):
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/README.md  (new)
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/seed_index.py  (new)
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/tool-mock/server.ts  (new)
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/examples/stepwise-orch.ts  (new)
source.extra_description_continued:
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/examples/event-saga.ts  (new)
- quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/ci/smoke-runner.ts  (new)
- docs/handbook/rag-orchestration-smokehouse.md (new)
- small package.json / ci config updates to wire the smoke runner into the quickstart CI (modified)

Snippet (example run):
1) seed a local index: `python quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/seed_index.py --dir ./data --seed 42`
2) start the tool mock: `node quickstarts/rag-smokehouse/tool-mock/server.js`
3) run the smoke runner (local): `npm --prefix quickstarts/rag-smokehouse run smoke`

A minimal orchestration YAML (illustrative):
```
name: invoice-reconcile
steps:
  - id: retrieve_context
    kind: retrieve
    index: ./data/index
  - id: call_invoice_tool
    kind: tool_call
    tool: http://localhost:8080/invoice-validate
  - id: finalize
    kind: generate
    template: templates/invoice-finalize.hbs
```

Testing and CI: the smoke runner boots a pinned dev model (via a test stub that returns deterministic tokens), mounts the seeded index, and runs the three scenarios. Assertions are intentionally strict for smoke CI (exact JSON keys + types) to catch regressions in structured output and tool-binding behavior. The runner is idempotent and writes an artifacts/ directory on success for later inspection.

Review conversation (condensed):
- Diego Morales: "Please add a short troubleshooting subsection on model timeouts and KV cache warmup — teams will hit this locally."
  - Ava Chen: "Added a troubleshooting subsection and a --cache-warmup flag to the smoke runner; documented warmup guidance."
- Priya Nair: "Docs look good; can we reduce required node version to 18.x? Some customers still use 18."
  - Ava Chen: "Dropped 20-only APIs; CI uses node 18 in the quickstart workflow now."
- Samir Gupta: "Nit: rename 'invoice-tool-call' to 'call_invoice_tool' for consistency with existing step ids."
  - Ava Chen: "Renamed and updated fixtures accordingly."
- Diego Morales (approval): "LGTM after CI passes and one minor doc tweak."

CI: first run showed a flaky test in smoke-runner (timing-sensitive). I adjusted a backoff and retried; final CI run passed all checks. Included CI summary: build: pass, lint: pass, smoke-e2e: pass, package: pass.

Why this is different from other quickstarts: rather than focusing on a single language runtime or a heavy authoring tool, Smokehouse is intentionally small (approx 300 LOC total for examples) and CI-first — it demonstrates how to keep RAG orchestrations testable and safe for rollouts. The quickstart emphasizes reproducibility (seeded index + deterministic model stub) and includes a pattern for validating tool outputs before you roll to production.

Guidance for maintainers / next steps:
- Consider adding a dedicated GH Action that runs the smoke runner against the latest compatible image on release branches (I left a TODO in docs/handbook).
- Potential follow-ups: add a Python variant of the runner, add an examples/telemetry-presets.json file that configures probes for latency and token-costs.

Merge: squash and merge. Merge rationale: small self-contained quickstart with docs and CI, had approvals from reviewers and green CI.

Notes: Linear ENG-2198 tracks wider authoring initiative; SUP-4123 links a support ticket where a customer requested reproducible smoke tests for their RAG rollout.

source.title: Introduce RAG rehearsal sandbox with step-mocks and eval hooks
source.author: Priya Nair
source.description:
Motivation: Add a small, local-first RAG "rehearsal" quickstart that helps engineers and evaluators iterate on end-to-end scenarios deterministically. The sandbox provides: 1) a scenario YAML format for stepwise interactions (queries, tool calls, agent decisions), 2) step-mock harnesses to replace external tools (search, embeddings, vector stores) with canned responses, 3) a lightweight CLI runner to replay scenarios locally and capture structured traces, and 4) eval hooks that emit run-level metrics and Golden-expected assertions for CI integration.

This PR introduces the example project, docs, and a test harness.

Checklist:
- [x] Add quickstart scaffolding and README
- [x] Implement scenario schema and runner CLI
- [x] Provide three sample scenarios (happy path, ambiguous retrieval, safety-fallback)

source.title: introduce-rag-llm-bridge-authoring-slate-quickstart
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description:
Context and motivation: Teams building retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) workflows often need a minimal, opinionated authoring surface that ties together prompt templates, tool contracts, structured output validators, and live telemetry presets. This quickstart ("LLM Bridge Authoring Slate"

What this PR adds: a compact developer playground and recipe that demonstrates: 1) contract-first tool declarations (OpenAPI/JSON Schema style) that the RAG runtime can call safely; 2) a lightweight authoring slate UI for editing prompt templates and live-previewing structured outputs; 3) a small sample indexer and retrieval shim (sqlite + simple vectorizer) for local experiments; 4) telemetry presets (token/response latency breakdown, tool-call traces, schema validation failures) wired to the Console dev-mode; and 5) a step-by-step README and end-to-end smoke tests for CI.

Why this is different: instead of a broad feature-complete sample, this quickstart focuses on choreography: how prompt variants, tool contracts, and structured-output validators interact during authoring. It intentionally keeps infra minimal so engineers can iterate locally and port patterns into Dedicated/Private deployments.

Checklist (author):
- [x] add quickstart skeleton under quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate (README, sample code, UI stubs)
- [x] implement contract-first tool adapter and example connector (tools/email-send.json, tools/search-contract.json)
- [x] add structured-output linter and example schema (schemas/action-trace.json)
- [x] wire minimal telemetry probes and Console integration guide
- [x] add smoke e2e test (ci/smoke-llm-bridge.yml) and basic unit tests for the retrieval shim

Summary of commits:
- init: add llm-bridge-slate quickstart skeleton and README
- feat: contract-first tool adapter + example contracts
- feat: authoring slate UI (static devpage) and preview server
- test: add smoke CI workflow and unit tests for retrieval shim
- docs: update quickstarts index and add Console dev-mode integration instructions

Changed areas/files (high-level):
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/README.md (new)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/app/ (new: small express/fastapi style preview server, UI assets)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/tools/search-contract.json (new)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/tools/email-send.json (new)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/schemas/action-trace.json (new)
- quickstarts/llm-bridge-slate/telemetry-presets.json (new)
- ci/smoke-llm-bridge.yml (new)
- docs/quickstarts/index.md (tweaked)

Small illustrative snippet added to the tool adapter (contract-first): tools/search-contract.json shows a description and a JSON Schema for the expected inputs; caller-side glue validates the response against schemas/action-trace.json before emitting traces to telemetry. No production credentials or secrets are included.

Review conversation highlights:
- Samir: "Nice focused surface — can we make the schema failure path more visible in the Console preset?"
- Maya: "Added an explicit 'schema-failure' trace type and an example in telemetry-presets.json; updated README to show how to toggle dev-mode traces."
- Joanna: "CI was flaky on the first run due to sqlite lock on macos-latest runner. Consider a retry/backoff."
- Maya: "Added lock-and-retry to the retrieval shim init and bumped the timeout in the smoke workflow."
- Ethan: "Docs wording looks good, but let's ensure we reference the runtime routing policy when describing canary-to-dedicated transitions."
- Maya: "Added a note under 'porting to Dedicated' section linking to runtime policies doc (docs/routing.md)."

CI and checks: First CI run failed on the smoke e2e due to sqlite concurrency, rerun passed. Linting and unit tests pass. The smoke workflow is conservative (runs on push to feature branch) and gated behind a quickstart label for the repository.

Merge outcome: merged via squash-merge after approvals from Samir and Joanna and green CI.

Usage and next steps: The README contains a local quickstart (npm run dev / python preview_server.py), a guided exercise to edit the tool contract and see the structured-output lint fire, and a short recipe for wiring the quickstart to a Redwood hosted API key for developers who want to test real model calls. Future follow-ups (tracked in ENG-7192): Dockerized dev container for offline authoring, additional example connectors (calendar, github-issue), and a Canary > Dedicated migration note with concrete config examples.

source.title: introduce-stateful-rag-finite-state-machine-quickstart
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a new quickstart demonstrating a finite-state-machine-driven RAG orchestration for stateful sessions, includes authoring guide, example code, and lightweight smoke CI.
source.description: Context & motivation: Many teams want RAG flows that maintain explicit conversational state across turns (session phases, latent tool state, multi-step confirmations) without shipping a heavyweight orchestrator. This PR introduces a compact Quickstart that wires a finite-state-machine (FSM) pattern to Redwood's tool-calling and incremental index APIs so developers can bootstrap stateful RAG sessions with clear guardrails and reproducible telemetry. Summary of changes: 1) new quickstart: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/ containing a README, TypeScript sample runner, an auth-free local dev shim, and a test harness; 2) authoring doc: docs/authoring/stateful-rag-fsm.md with design rationale and recommended prompt templates; 3) telemetry presets: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/telemetry-presets.json for Console probe ingestion; 4) smoke CI workflow: .github/workflows/qst-stateful-fsm-smoke.yml to validate local run in CI; 5) small SDK helper: packages/sdk-utils/src/fsm.ts (new lightweight FSM utilities) 6) tests: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/__tests__/fsm-smoke.test.ts High-level commit summary: - Initial quickstart scaffold and README. - Add FSM runtime helpers and inline JSDoc + types. - Add authoring guide with example prompts and guardrails. - Add telemetry presets and console link example. - Add GitHub smoke workflow and a minimal unit test. Changed areas/files (high level): quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/{README.md,runner.ts,telemetry-presets.json,__tests__/fsm-smoke.test.ts}, docs/authoring/stateful-rag-fsm.md, packages/sdk-utils/src/fsm.ts, .github/workflows/qst-stateful-fsm-smoke.yml Notable design points: - FSM model: explicit states (INIT, RETRIEVE, TOOL_CALL, CONFIRM, FINALIZE) and deterministic transitions on structured outputs. - Tool binding: demonstrates idempotent tool wrappers and a replay-safe invocation pattern using request-scoped tokens. - Caching: shows prefix KV caching for retrieval tokens between RETRIEVE->TOOL_CALL transitions to reduce repeated embedding calls. - Telemetry: emits per-state timers and transition tags consumed by Redwood Console via provided presets. Single-line summary of example runner behavior: The TS runner seeds an in-memory index, starts a session in INIT, emits a retrieval request, enters RETRIEVE, chooses candidate passages, optionally enters TOOL_CALL (e.g., call external knowledge API or custom search tool), moves to CONFIRM when candidate requires human-in-loop confirmation, and then FINALIZE to return structured output; the runner includes a --simulate-human flag for deterministic CI. Review conversation (high-level): Diego: "Please add an example of error path transition when retrieval returns empty; show fallback to open-domain generation." Author (Maya): "Added fallback transition with explicit prompt template and covered in authoring doc." Priya: "Telemetry presets look good; please ensure tags use kebab-case." Author: "Fixed tags and added unit test asserting telemetry fields." Samir (approval): "Works for me, tests and CI pass." CI checks: lint (pass), unit-tests (pass), smoke-ci-run (pass after one rerun for environment flakiness), docs-build (pass). Testing notes: smoke CI uses node 18 and a lightweight in-memory vector index, tests assert deterministic outputs using seeded RNG. Migration/compat notes: SDK helper fsm.ts is additive and exported from packages/sdk-utils index without breaking existing APIs. Rollout plan: ship as a Quickstart under the Quickstarts landing page with a short blurb and a linked authoring guide. Merge outcome: merged via squash-merge after approvals. How to review locally: 1) cd quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag 2) yarn install && yarn build 3) yarn run seed && yarn run start -- --simulate-human 4) run the smoke test: yarn test:fsm-smoke Why this is different from other RAG quickstarts: most quickstarts focus on tool-calling, debugging, or replay; this one centers an explicit FSM pattern and shows best practices for state transitions, telemetry tags per state, idempotent tool wrappers, and KV-backed retrieval caching. Related linear/Jira: ENG-2103 (FSM runtime utility), PM-812 (Quickstart deliverable). Checklist: [x] Add quickstart scaffold [x] Add authoring guide [x] Add telemetry presets [x] Add smoke CI [x] Add unit tests [x] Update packages/sdk-utils export [x] Ensure docs build clean [x] Add release notes entry Post-merge follow-ups: - Add a short walkthrough video to docs site (task assigned to PM-812). - Consider adding a Go translation of the runner if there is demand. Contact: Maya Chen (mchen@redwood.ai) for follow-ups and questions.

Summary of changes: 1) new quickstart: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/ containing a README, TypeScript sample runner, an auth-free local dev shim, and a test harness; 2) authoring doc: docs/authoring/stateful-rag-fsm.md with design rationale and recommended prompt templates; 3) telemetry presets: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/telemetry-presets.json for Console probe ingestion; 4) smoke CI workflow: .github/workflows/qst-stateful-fsm-smoke.yml to validate local run in CI; 5) small SDK helper: packages/sdk-utils/src/fsm.ts (new lightweight FSM utilities) 6) tests: quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/__tests__/fsm-smoke.test.ts High-level commit summary: - Initial quickstart scaffold and README. - Add FSM runtime helpers and inline JSDoc + types. - Add authoring guide with example prompts and guardrails. - Add telemetry presets and console link example. - Add GitHub smoke workflow and a minimal unit test. Changed areas/files (high level): quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag/{README.md,runner.ts,telemetry-presets.json,__tests__/fsm-smoke.test.ts}, docs/authoring/stateful-rag-fsm.md, packages/sdk-utils/src/fsm.ts, .github/workflows/qst-stateful-fsm-smoke.yml Notable design points: - FSM model: explicit states (INIT, RETRIEVE, TOOL_CALL, CONFIRM, FINALIZE) and deterministic transitions on structured outputs. - Tool binding: demonstrates idempotent tool wrappers and a replay-safe invocation pattern using request-scoped tokens. - Caching: shows prefix KV caching for retrieval tokens between RETRIEVE->TOOL_CALL transitions to reduce repeated embedding calls. - Telemetry: emits per-state timers and transition tags consumed by Redwood Console via provided presets. Single-line summary of example runner behavior: The TS runner seeds an in-memory index, starts a session in INIT, emits a retrieval request, enters RETRIEVE, chooses candidate passages, optionally enters TOOL_CALL (e.g., call external knowledge API or custom search tool), moves to CONFIRM when candidate requires human-in-loop confirmation, and then FINALIZE to return structured output; the runner includes a --simulate-human flag for deterministic CI. Review conversation (high-level): Diego: "Please add an example of error path transition when retrieval returns empty; show fallback to open-domain generation." Author (Maya): "Added fallback transition with explicit prompt template and covered in authoring doc." Priya: "Telemetry presets look good; please ensure tags use kebab-case." Author: "Fixed tags and added unit test asserting telemetry fields." Samir (approval): "Works for me, tests and CI pass." CI checks: lint (pass), unit-tests (pass), smoke-ci-run (pass after one rerun for environment flakiness), docs-build (pass). Testing notes: smoke CI uses node 18 and a lightweight in-memory vector index, tests assert deterministic outputs using seeded RNG. Migration/compat notes: SDK helper fsm.ts is additive and exported from packages/sdk-utils index without breaking existing APIs. Rollout plan: ship as a Quickstart under the Quickstarts landing page with a short blurb and a linked authoring guide. Merge outcome: merged via squash-merge after approvals. How to review locally: 1) cd quickstarts/stateful-fsm-rag 2) yarn install && yarn build 3) yarn run seed && yarn run start -- --simulate-human 4) run the smoke test: yarn test:fsm-smoke Why this is different from other RAG quickstarts: most quickstarts focus on tool-calling, debugging, or replay; this one centers an explicit FSM pattern and shows best practices for state transitions, telemetry tags per state, idempotent tool wrappers, and KV-backed retrieval caching. Related linear/Jira: ENG-2103 (FSM runtime utility), PM-812 (Quickstart deliverable). Checklist: [x] Add quickstart scaffold [x] Add authoring guide [x] Add telemetry presets [x] Add smoke CI [x] Add unit tests [x] Update packages/sdk-utils export [x] Ensure docs build clean [x] Add release notes entry Post-merge follow-ups: - Add a short walkthrough video to docs site (task assigned to PM-812). - Consider adding a Go translation of the runner if there is demand. Contact: Maya Chen (mchen@redwood.ai) for follow-ups and questions.

source.title: Add snippet-explorer worklet with typed telemetry hooks and runnable README
source.author: Aisha Rahman
source.body:
Motivation: shipping compact, runnable quickstarts that demonstrate structured tool-calling, streaming snippets, and minimal telemetry hooks has been a frequent ask from customer-facing teams. This PR introduces a new quickstart called 'snippet-explorer' — a small web worklet + Node runner that shows how to wire Redwood SDK streaming, tool calls, and typed telemetry probes into a developer playground.

What this PR contains: a) quickstarts/snippet-explorer/README.md with a one-minute intro, runnable steps (devcontainer, npm start, and a single smoke request). b) playlet/index.ts: lightweight worklet that runs a sample agent, exposes a compact UI stub and emits typed probe events. c) examples/snippet-explorer/telemetry-probes.yaml: example probe schema and recommended labels for integration with Console ingestion. d) docs/quickstarts/snippet-explorer.md: docs-site-ready copy with API snippets and common troubleshooting. e) ci/smoke-tests/snippet-explorer.yml: minimal CI smoke to ensure startup and sample request succeed in CI. f) devcontainer.json and package.json updates to make local onboarding one-command.

Design notes and tradeoffs: the worklet intentionally keeps dependencies minimal (no browser UI framework) so teams can copy-paste into embedded sandboxes. Telemetry hooks are typed (TypeScript interfaces) and intentionally shallow: event types: session.start, request.kv_cache_hit, tool.call, render.fragment. We opted to emit JSON-serializable objects only (no binary payloads) to minimize console-side ingestion complexity.

Usage instructions (excerpted from README): 1) git clone && cd quickstarts/snippet-explorer 2) code . (opens devcontainer) 3) npm install && npm run dev 4) open http://localhost:5173 and press 'Run sample' to see streaming tokens and a single tool call. The README also describes environment variables used by the quickstart (REDWOOD_API_KEY, REDWOOD_REGION) and fallbacks for local dev (embedded stub tokens).

Testing and CI: smoke test runs the devserver in headless mode and sends a single sample request verifying: (1) streaming returns at least one delta, (2) the expected tool call was invoked (mock), (3) telemetry probe for session.start and tool.call were emitted to a local file sink. CI passes are reported as part of this PR.

Backward compatibility and rollout: this is an additive quickstart — no breaking change to existing quickstarts. Consumers can import the typed probe interfaces from the example package for local testing; we do not publish a new package.

Follow-ups: 1) Integrate a Console dashboard template that imports examples/snippet-explorer/telemetry-probes.yaml (tracked in ENG-1045). 2) Add a variant that uses Dedicated autoscaling example (ENG-1070).

Checklist: - [x] README and docs copy - [x] Runnable devcontainer + npm scripts - [x] Telemetry probe schema and typed hooks - [x] Minimal CI smoke - [x] Reviewer feedback addressed

Related: Linear ENG-1023 (quickstart backlog), PM-78 (customer ask).

Commits summary: see commits field for high-level commit messages.
commits: a1f4b6c: scaffold snippet-explorer quickstart and README
b2d77f1: add playlet runtime and example tool adapter (mock)
c3e8a2d: wire typed telemetry hooks and probe schema yml
d4f9b33: add devcontainer and package.json scripts for one-click dev
e5aa112: add CI smoke test and small docs edits
f6bb223: address reviewer comments (env docs, test retries)
review_comments: Priya Natarajan: Please document required env vars and add a short troubleshooting section for CORS and local stub tokens. Also suggest a tiny unit test for the probe emitter — it's helpful for future refactors.
Author (Aisha): Added env var table and troubleshooting section; added a file-sink probe unit test and retry in the CI smoke to reduce flakes.
Eli Turner: The telemetry schema looks good but please add a recommended sampling guidance to the YAML to avoid high-cardinality ingestion in Console.
Author (Aisha): Added sampling guidance comment and a recommended label set in telemetry-probes.yaml. Updated README to call out cost considerations.
Marco Silva: Verified local devflow in the devcontainer, smoke CI passed locally. Approving after minor wording nit in docs fixed.
Marco Silva: APPROVED — will squash+merge once CI completes.

source.title: Add RAG telemetry playback minirunner quickstart and docs
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: Reproducible debugging of RAG sessions requires the ability to replay request/response and telemetry traces locally. This quickstart introduces a lightweight "minirunner" that replays recorded telemetry and request traces into a local RAG dev environment so engineers and SREs can reproduce quality regressions, latency spikes, and tool-calling failures without needing a full cluster.

What this PR adds: a) a minimal Go-based playback worker (examples/playback-minirunner/) that consumes Redwood-formatted trace bundles and emits the same sequence of API calls and tool invocations to a developer's local stack; b) a small TypeScript shim (examples/ts-playback-proxy/) that simulates the tool adapter surface for browser examples; c) a step-by-step quickstart (quickstarts/rag-telemetry-playback/README.md) with sample trace bundles, CLI usage, and troubleshooting tips; d) docs site snippet (docs/quickstarts/rag-telemetry-playback.md) and a live example reference under examples/sample-traces/.

Design notes: the minirunner is intentionally opinionated and small: deterministic replay (timestamp-preserving or accelerated modes), configurable injection of network latency and token-level delays, deterministic seeding for RNG-affecting model calls, and an assertions harness that allows authors to attach simple post-replay checks for expected outputs. Playback bundles are newline-delimited JSON events (NDJSON) matching our telemetry export format; the runner supports compressed (.ndjson.gz) bundles out of the box. The TS proxy is a deliberate high-level stub to make it trivial to wire browser-based examples into the same replay harness.

Checklist:
- [x] Implement Go minirunner with replay and latency simulation
- [x] TS proxy and two sample adapters (tool-echo, external-search-stub)
- [x] Quickstart README and stepwise tutorial with screenshots
- [x] Docs site quickstart page + sidebar entry
- [x] Unit tests for replay ordering and RNG seeding
- [x] CI integration and a small e2e smoke test using sample bundle

Compatibility: the runner targets the Redwood telemetry v1 schema; it will gracefully reject older bundles with a helpful error message and link to the migration guide. It does not depend on private infra and is safe to run on developer machines.

How to try locally (short): git checkout HEAD && cd examples/playback-minirunner && make build && ./playback-minirunner --bundle ../../examples/sample-traces/simple-rag-session.ndjson.gz --target http://localhost:8080 --accelerate 4x --assert assertions.yml

Related work: this addresses recurring developer pain surfaced in ENG-1723 and pairs with the rag session recorder introduced earlier. It will be useful for on-call reproduction, authoring regression tests, and for documentation examples that show exact event flows.

Merge notes: squash-and-merge after approvals. CI had one intermittent test failure (telemetry/serializer) on the first run; that was fixed by normalizing timestamp parsing in commit 3.
commits: a1f3e9d: add initial playback-minirunner binary and core replay engine
b2c4d77: add NDJSON parser and gzip support; add latency injection hooks
c3d5e88: add TypeScript proxy shim and sample adapters
d4e6f99: quickstart README + docs site page + sample bundles
e5f7a00: tests: add unit tests for ordering and RNG seeding; fix serializer timestamp parsing
review_comments: Diego Ramirez: Nice focused quickstart — can we make the default bundle path relative to the repo root in the README so it's easier for cross-os users?
Ava Chen (author): Good call — updated README to use ./examples/sample-traces and added notes for Windows path handling (backslash vs forward-slash) in commit e5f7a00.
Priya Nair: The assertion harness is useful; can we document the assert DSL a bit more and add one failing assertion example so authors learn the failure mode?
Ava Chen (author): Added an assertions.yml.example and a short subsection showing a failing assertion and how to inspect the replay trace (commit d4e6f99).

Markus Lindgren: CI showed the telemetry serializer failure on first run — happy to approve once tests are stable.

Ava Chen (author): Fixed timestamp parsing and normalized timezone handling; re-ran CI and green.
source.release_notes: Add telemetry playback minirunner quickstart: local replay tooling to reproduce RAG sessions, sample trace bundles, TS proxy adapter, and docs. Useful for debugging, on-call reproduction, and authoring regression tests.

source.title: Introduce RAG Navigator: lightweight interactive playground and CLI walkthrough
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.release_notes: Add a compact interactive RAG playground called 'RAG Navigator' with a minimal CLI, browser devserver, and opinionated telemetry probes for quick local experimentation; targeted at developer onboarding and internal workshops.
source.description: Context and motivation: teams keep asking for a tiny, batteries-included RAG playground that lets new engineers run a retrieval+agent loop locally with minimal setup and observable telemetry. This PR adds 'RAG Navigator', a lightweight quickstart that combines: 1) a minimal Node-based CLI to scaffold an index and seed prompts; 2) a small devserver that serves a compact interactive UI for stepwise inspection of agent decisions and tool calls; 3) opinionated telemetry probes that emit structured traces (local file/console) for step-level assertions; 4) example prompt templates and a guided walkthrough README. Checklist: - [x] scaffold CLI and devserver - [x] add telemetry probes and sample assertions - [x] provide two example corpora (kb-small, kb-news) - [x] include CI smoke tests and offline index fixture - [x] add docs quickstart page and links to examples gallery Implementation notes: the CLI is intentionally minimal (no auth required) and targets local-first experimentation; it supports `nav init`, `nav index --corpus=kb-small`, `nav run --scenario=tutor`, and `nav export-session`. The devserver runs on :5173, serves a compact single-page authoring UI with stepwise trace view and a tool-call inspector. Telemetry format is newline-delimited JSON to simplify ingestion by local eval harnesses. Security: playground is explicitly for dev use; README warns about not exposing to public networks. Related work: follows conventions from the authoring-studio and step-replayer projects but is intentionally compact for workshops (see ENG-4821). Testing: added a CI job that runs `nav index` against a bundled offline fixture and executes a headless smoke-run that asserts the agent reaches an expected final step. source.title: add-interactive-rag-kata-telemetry-workbench-and-authoring-sandbox-quickstart
source.author: Priya Singh
source.release_notes: Adds an interactive RAG authoring sandbox (live-reload) with three kata scenarios, telemetry probes wired to the Redwood Console workbench, and e2e smoke tests and docs.
source.summary: Interactive RAG authoring quickstart that teaches common developer patterns via small 'kata' exercises and exposes telemetry probes to the Console workbench.
source.description:
Context: New developer-onboarding quickstart to accelerate RAG authoring and telemetry adoption. The goal is to provide a compact, hands-on sandbox with stepwise exercises (katas) that teach: setting up a local index, wiring a retrieval tool, authoring prompts with structured output checks, and instrumenting telemetry probes for latency/cost/quality. This PR delivers the sandbox app, three kata scenarios, a telemetry probes package, docs site updates, and CI smoke tests.

Motivation: Engineering teams repeatedly ask for reproducible, minimal examples focused on both correctness (assertions/step checks) and observability. Existing quickstarts teach authoring but omit live telemetry wiring or stepwise kata-style learning. This PR fills that gap and provides a low-friction path to try telemetry workbench features paired with RAG.

Checklist: - [x] Add interactive sandbox app with live-reload (local dev container) - [x] Add three kata scenarios (indexing, tool-calling, structured-output assertions) - [x] Add telemetry probe definitions and Console workbench integration - [x] Add lightweight e2e smoke tests + CI job - [x] Docs: quickstart page, inline walkthrough, and screenshots - [x] Add examples for Python and TypeScript client usage - [x] Add short authoring exercises and validation harness

What changed (high level): commits summarize below. Key deliverables: a new quickstart under quickstarts/rag-kata, telemetry/probes/kata-probe.yaml, docs/quickstarts/rag-kata.md, authoring-sandbox web app (React + local shim), and CI smoke workflow ci/smoke/rag-kata-smoke.yml. Added three sample scenarios in examples/rag-kata-scenarios (small JSON indexes + prompts).

Commits summary: see commits field for commit messages. Highlights: initial sandbox scaffold, add kata scenarios and sample indexes, implement telemetry probe templates and Console wiring, add e2e smoke tests and CI job, docs and screenshots, fix test flakiness (retry/backoff), polish CLI helper rag-kata.

Files changed (high level): quickstarts/rag-kata/README.md; quickstarts/rag-kata/devcontainer.json; quickstarts/rag-kata/sandbox/Dockerfile; quickstarts/rag-kata/sandbox/src/*; quickstarts/rag-kata/cli/rag-kata (executable stub); docs/quickstarts/rag-kata.md; telemetry/probes/kata-probe.yaml; examples/rag-kata-scenarios/{index.json,prompts.json,assertions.json}; ci/smoke/rag-kata-smoke.yml; tests/e2e/rag-kata.spec.ts; package.json updates for authoring-sandbox.

Review conversation (summary): - Claire: requested clearer failure messages for step assertions and a small refactor to the telemetry probe labels for dashboard filtering. - Priya: addressed with commit "refactor: normalize probe labels for dashboard" and updated docs with examples. - Diego: asked for an e2e smoke job and a compact CI matrix (node12/node16) — added CI job and pinned a single Node version to reduce flakiness. - Ethan: suggested sample prompts to demonstrate tool-fallback behavior and added an additional kata to exercise fallback. All reviewers approved.

CI notes: First CI run had one failing test (flaky timing in e2e related to local index warmup). Reproduced locally, added retry/backoff and a small wait in the devcontainer start script. Re-run passed; CI status is green for all checks.

Merge: Squash merge into main. Follow-up tasks: add more katas for embeddings-first flows (ENG-3560) and a dedicated telemetry recipe for Dedicated deployments. Links: Linear: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-3412, Quickstart preview: /quickstarts/rag-kata (rendered), Doc PR reference: docs-site#preview.

Notes for maintainers: The sandbox ships a tiny indexed corpus (~20 docs); do not include production data. The telemetry probe YAML is safe to apply in staging; namespaced by quickstart: "rag-kata". If you need to revert, the squash commit is titled 'add rag kata telemetry sandbox' and can be reverted at main.

source.title: Add RAG tool-bound workflow quickstart with stubbed agent runtime
source.author: Asha Kapoor
source.release_notes: New quickstart: a minimal, tool-bound RAG authoring sandbox with a stubbed agent runtime, structured-output contracts, and smoke CI for local validation.
source.description: Motivation: provide a small, battery-included quickstart for teams authoring retrieval-augmented tool-calling agents that need fast local feedback loops. This quickstart packages: 1) a tiny stubbed agent runtime (TypeScript) that runs locally and exposes a tool-binding API; 2) a set of contract-first tool schemas and structured-output validators; 3) an incremental index + mocked retriever that can be swapped for a real vector DB; 4) telemetry probe scaffolds and a smoke test harness for CI. The goal is to let engineers iterate on tool contracts and prompt/tool orchestration without provisioning full infra or real connectors. What this PR changes: - Adds quickstarts/toolbound-agent/README.md with step-by-step authoring, troubleshooting, and intended learning objectives. - Adds an opinionated example agent at quickstarts/toolbound-agent/examples/agent.ts demonstrating tool discovery, deterministic replay, and partial-response resume. - Adds a compact mock retriever and incremental indexer (quickstarts/toolbound-agent/lib/indexer.ts) and a CLI (dev/runner.ts) to run scenarios offline. - Adds structured output schema examples (schemas/tool-contracts.json) with a lightweight validator and linter integration. - Adds CI smoke pipeline config (.github/workflows/quickstart-ci.yml) that runs headless scenario replay and the structured-output linter. - Adds telemetry probe presets (telemetry/probes.yaml) and docs page (docs/rag-toolbound.md) describing recommended metrics and alert thresholds. Tests and verification: ran the local runner across three seed scenarios (happy path, tool-misuse, partial response resume) and exercised the structured-output linter. CI: all checks pass in the quickstart pipeline (smoke tests + linter). Checklist: - [x] Quickstart README with usage and learning objectives - [x] Example agent demonstrating tool-binding and structured-output - [x] Mock retriever and incremental index for offline iteration - [x] Structured-output schema + lint integration - [x] CI smoke workflow for reproducible validation - [x] Telemetry probe presets and docs link Testing notes and tips: runner seeds are intentionally small (≤ 3 documents) to keep latency low. To integrate with a real index swap the 'MockRetriever' in examples/agent.ts; instructions included in README. Related work: this quickstart is intentionally narrower than the full RAG Playbook quickstarts — it complements the existing RAG templates by focusing on contract-first tool-binding authoring and offline validation. Linked Linear: ENG-4232 (authoring DX), PM-987 (quickstart intake). Merge: Squash-merged after two rounds of review and one CI re-run. Known limitations: no cloud indexer included; the runner is not production-grade and is intended for authoring/eval only. Future work: add a Rust/WASM minimal runner and a Python adapter for teams who prefer those stacks.
source.commits_summary: 3 commits: initial quickstart scaffold; add structured-output schemas and linter; CI smoke pipeline and docs update
review_conversation: Diego Alvarez (reviewer): Nice compact surface. Can we make the validator pluggable so teams can swap JSON Schema for TypeBox? Requested change: expose a Validator interface and default adapter.
Asha Kapoor (author): Good call — added Validator interface in lib/schema_validator.ts and default JSON Schema adapter. Documented how to replace it in README.
Maya Chen (reviewer): Telemetry presets look solid. Suggest lowering request tracing sample rate in probes.yaml to 0.1 to keep local storage drops low.
Asha Kapoor (author): Reduced tracing sample rate and added a note about toggling for lab vs. smoke CI.
Ethan Park (reviewer): CI flake observed on the first run (node 18 vs 20 mismatch). Requested change: pin the node version in the action.
author_replies: Asha Kapoor: Pinned node to 20.x in quickstart-ci.yml and re-ran CI. Added a troubleshooting section in README about node/npm mismatch.
Asha Kapoor: Added usage snippet showing index swap: replace MockRetriever with RedwoodVectorRetriever from examples comments.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash on 2026-01-27 by Maya Chen

source.title: Add “Hello world” first request snippet aligned with activation CTAs
source.author: Dylan Brooks
source.release_notes: Adds a minimal “first successful request” snippet (curl + Node + Python) that matches the onboarding email/console CTA and reduces time-to-first-token for new Hosted API users.
source.body:
## Context
This sprint’s primary activation KPI is “first successful request” and “first 100k tokens” within 7 days. We’ve seen drop-off at the earliest step due to (a) too many options in the quickstart, (b) mismatch between lifecycle email CTAs and what the docs show, and (c) missing/unclear env var and model defaults.

This PR adds a minimal, copy/paste “Hello world” snippet that matches the onboarding email v2 + console onboarding checklist CTA wording: “Make your first request.” The snippet is intentionally small: create API key → export env var → single streaming request.

## What changed
- New `hello-world` page that is optimized for TTFT (goal: <2 minutes from API key creation to first streamed token).
- Adds 3 variants with identical structure:
  - curl (streaming)
  - Node/TypeScript (SDK)
  - Python (SDK)
- Standardizes naming used across growth surfaces:
  - Env var: `REDWOOD_API_KEY`
  - Base URL: `https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1`
  - Model example: `redwood/verified-llm-small` (placeholder here is a stable, low-latency model option used in onboarding)
- Adds a small “Common errors” callout focused on the top early failures we’ve seen (missing key / 401, model typo / 404, network / proxy).
- Adds UTM-safe anchors for lifecycle email deep links and console CTA targets (no JS; just stable fragment ids).

## Example (curl)
```bash
export REDWOOD_API_KEY="<your_api_key>"

curl https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/verified-llm-small",
    "stream": true,
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Say hello in one sentence."}]
  }'
```

## Measurement / instrumentation notes
- This repo doesn’t emit product events, but the snippet is designed to correspond cleanly to funnel events being instrumented in the API/console (ENG-10492): `api_key_created` → `first_request_succeeded`.
- The page includes consistent CTA copy to reduce confusion in qualitative feedback and support tickets.

## QA

title: Add “Hello world” first request snippet aligned with activation CTAs
preview:
## Context
This sprint’s primary activation KPI is “first successful request” and “first 100k tokens” within 7 days. We’ve seen drop-off at the earliest step due to (a) too many options in the quickstart, (b) mismatch between lifecycle email CTAs and what the docs show, and (c) missing/unclear env var and model defaults.

This PR adds a minimal, copy/paste “Hello world” snippet that matches the onboarding email v2 + console onboarding checklist CTA wording: “Make your first request.” The snippet is intentionally small: create API key → export env var → single streaming request.

## What changed
- New `hello-world` page that is optimized for TTFT (goal: <2 minutes from API key creation to first streamed token).
- Adds 3 variants with identical structure:
  - curl (streaming)
  - Node/TypeScript (SDK)
  - Python (SDK)
- Standardizes naming used across growth surfaces:
  - Env var: `REDWOOD_API_KEY`
  - Base URL: `https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1`
  - Model example: `redwood/verified-llm-small` (placeholder here is a stable, low-latency model option used in onboarding)
- Adds a small “Common errors” callout focused on the top early failures we’ve seen (missing key / 401, model typo / 404, network / proxy).
- Adds UTM-safe anchors for lifecycle email deep links and console CTA targets (no JS; just stable fragment ids).

## Example (curl)
```bash
export REDWOOD_API_KEY="<your_api_key>"

curl https://api.redwoodinference.com/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "redwood/verified-llm-small",
    "stream": true,
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Say hello in one sentence."}]
  }'
```

## Measurement / instrumentation notes
- This repo doesn’t emit product events, but the snippet is designed to correspond cleanly to funnel events being instrumented in the API/console (ENG-10492): `api_key_created` → `first_request_succeeded`.
- The page includes consistent CTA copy to reduce confusion in qualitative feedback and support tickets.

## QA
- Verified commands work end-to-end against staging.
- Ensured snippets are mobile-friendly (short lines, minimal wrapping).
- Confirmed no mention of experimental features or non-default flags.

## Rollout
- Safe to merge immediately; Growth will link to this page in lifecycle email v2 + console nudge CTAs via existing docs PRs.

## Screenshots
- N/A (markdown-only change)

source.title: Add RAG assertion workshop and offline eval kit
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a compact, local-first RAG workshop and offline evaluation kit. Includes step-by-step authoring guide, assertion DSL, telemetry probe presets, and CI smoke job to validate regressions before release.
source.description:
Motivation: Engineers building retrieval-augmented agents need reproducible, offline workflows to author prompt/knowledge assertions and validate regressions without spinning up hosted infra. This quickstart provides a compact workshop that demonstrates assertion-driven evals, example agent tool-calling patterns, and telemetry probes that map errors to actionable traces.

What this PR adds: - a guided workshop README with hands-on tasks and expected outcomes - a tiny assertion DSL and runner (authorable JSON/YAML assertion files) to express functional and quality checks - an offline eval harness that spins a local retriever + lightweight model shim for fast iteration - telemetry probe presets (span names, tags, and sampling hints) and a small dashboard template - CI smoke job to run the assertion suite on PRs so regressions are caught early

Key usage notes: 1) Clone the quickstart and follow the README at quickstarts/rag-assertion-workshop/README.md for a 20-minute workshop. 2) To run locally: sh scripts/run-local-eval.sh --index ./data/small-corpus.json --assertions assertions/examples.yaml 3) To add assertions: author a new entry in assertions/ with description, input, expected retrieval top-k, and a fuzzy-match quality threshold. 4) Telemetry: add TELEMETRY_ENDPOINT to env and use the provided probes.yaml to map spans to workshop steps.

Checklist: - [x] Add README + step tasks - [x] Implement assertion runner and examples - [x] Add telemetry presets and dashboard skeleton - [x] Add CI smoke job and local run script - [x] Wire minimal example agent with tool-calling stub - [x] Tests: unit tests for assertion runner, end-to-end offline smoke run

Related: ENG-3921 (authoring-first evals), PM-684 (workshop materials), SUP-1205 (customer request for offline regression checks). Docs preview: https://docs.redwood.com/quickstarts/rag-assertion-workshop

Notes on scope: Designed to be small and local-first — it intentionally uses a model shim to keep costs low for teams onboarding. The toolkit is extensible and includes pointers for switching to a production-backed retriever or Redwood-hosted models.

source.title: interactive rag scenarios: tool timeouts and resilient fallbacks quickstart
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Adds an interactive quickstart demonstrating robust RAG agent patterns when calling external tools: per-tool timeout and retry policies, deterministic fallback strategies (local cache / degraded-mode answers), session replay for authoring and debugging, and lightweight telemetry probes for latency-sensitive deployments. The quickstart includes a compact dev server to run scenarios locally, stepwise assertions for authoring test cases, and CI smoke tests that validate timeouts and fallback behavior. Motivated by ENG-1843 and on-call feedback about tool stalls increasing MTTR; this quickstart is aimed at engineers evaluating production guardrails for tool-calling agents and improves observability during local dev and CI verification.

source.title: Introduce RAG step-factory playbook and lightweight e2e devkit
source.author: Anika Rao
source.description: Context and motivation: Teams repeatedly ask for a compact, production-adjacent RAG quickstart that (1) defines reusable step templates for common pipeline stages (query rewrite, candidate fetch, rerank, synthesis), (2) ships a tiny devkit for deterministic e2e runs and replayable telemetry, and (3) demonstrates a connector pattern (GraphQL) used in real products. This PR introduces the 'step-factory' playbook: a curated set of YAML step templates, a node-based devkit runner with a lightweight in-memory index/replay harness, and docs + smoke CI to make authoring and testing RAG flows straightforward. What this contains (high level): - playbooks/step-factory/README.md with conceptual overview and authoring patterns - playbooks/step-factory/templates/* (5 canonical step templates: rewrite, retrieve, rerank, synthesize, commit) - devkit/runner/index.ts: small runner that composes steps, exposes a CLI (rw-step run --playbook playbooks/step-factory/demo.yaml) - devkit/replay/probes.ts: deterministic replay harness and step-level assertion helpers - examples/graphql-connector/sample-connector.ts: example adapter showing connector->tool-call pattern and auth stub - docs/site/content/quickstarts/step-factory.md: full quickstart for docs site with config snippets and troubleshooting - tests/e2e/step-factory.spec.ts: end-to-end smoke with seeded index and step assertions - ci/workflows/quickstart-smoke.yml: nightly smoke and PR run (fast mode) Implementation notes: - Step templates are small YAML fragments (no infra assumptions). They reference minimal tool contracts (input/outputs) so teams can adapt them to Dedicated/Private deploys. - The devkit runner intentionally avoids heavy deps; it supports local in-memory corpora and a simple HTTP-based mock connector for external tools. - Replay harness records step inputs/outputs and asserts invariants (token budget, latency upper bound, structured-output schemas). - Example GraphQL connector demonstrates incremental indexing and delta-sync semantics for demo data sets. Migration and compatibility: - Non-breaking: templates are additive and the runner is opt-in. - If teams adopt the runner, they may want to replace the in-memory index with their existing vector store; the README includes migration pointers. Checklist (what I did): - [x] Add playbook templates and demo playbook - [x] Implement lightweight devkit runner + replay probes - [x] Add example GraphQL connector and auth stub - [x] Docs quickstart page + local CLI usage - [x] E2E smoke tests and CI workflow - [x] Add linear links and release notes Drafting notes: Linear ENG-8642 contains the design doc and acceptance criteria. See demo recording link (internal docs) in the Linear ticket. This description is intentionally long to aid reviewers and release notes.

See docs/quickstarts/step-factory for usage and migration notes.
