source.author: Wei Chen
source.description: This adds a new end-to-end “Production Readiness” quickstart intended to mirror the Console onboarding / first production launch checklist. The quickstart walks a new Hosted API user from: API key setup → first request → streaming → retries/timeouts → usage visibility → quotas/budgets guardrails → basic observability hooks → model pinning + fallback (conceptual; uses config placeholders). Motivation: we keep seeing early prod launches fail on 401/403 setup mistakes, streaming disconnect confusion, and 429 surprises; the checklist in Console helps, but we need a canonical runnable path customers can copy/paste and deploy. Scope: adds a new quickstart directory with Python + TypeScript examples (Go is stubbed with TODO and links to the SDK guide), adds a minimal “healthcheck” endpoint pattern, structured logs, and a tiny dashboard note for usage-lag expectations. Includes guidance for where to configure quotas/budgets in Console and links out to Terraform sample budget alerts. Non-goals: full guided tour, Dedicated/Private specifics, deep integration with the checklist progress service (this is docs/examples only).
commits: quickstarts: add production-readiness scaffold + README
python: add prod-ready chat + streaming + retry presets
typescript: add streaming example + reconnect notes + structured logging
docs: link out to quotas/budgets + usage dashboard expectations
ci: add lightweight smoke tests for new quickstart
notable_snippets: Python (timeouts/retries): client = RedwoodClient(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"], timeout_s=30, max_retries=3, backoff_initial_s=0.5, backoff_max_s=8.0)

TypeScript (streaming with reconnect note): for await (const event of client.chat.completions.stream({...})) { /* handle deltas */ } // if connection drops mid-stream, treat as non-idempotent and retry only when safe (see README)
Structured logging fields: request_id, model, route, status_code, latency_ms, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens
source.test_plan: 1) Local Python: export REDWOOD_API_KEY=...; cd production-readiness/python; python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt; python app/main.py --non-streaming; python app/main.py --streaming. 2) Local TypeScript: cd production-readiness/typescript; npm ci; npm run start -- --non-streaming; npm run start -- --streaming. 3) Trigger 429 behavior: set low per-minute limit in Console (or use provided instructions to request temporary low quota), run load script in README, confirm exponential backoff and log output. 4) Observability: confirm logs include request_id + latency_ms; optionally set OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT and run sample docker-compose from observability.md. 5) CI smoke: workflow runs dependency install + one non-streaming request behind a secret-less stub (mocked transport) to ensure compilation + basic execution paths don’t regress.
source.docs_and_checklist_alignment: Checklist mapping is called out in production-readiness/README.md under “Checklist parity”. Steps covered: API key + env vars, first successful request, streaming, retries/timeouts, usage dashboard + expected lag, quotas/budgets basics, basic observability hooks, model pinning guidance + fallback policy concepts (links to docs; no server-side config changes here).
screenshots: N/A (README-driven quickstart). Sample output blocks included in README for both languages.
review_conversation: Monica Patel (review): Can we ensure we’re using the new recommended retry presets language from ENG-5820? Also please be explicit that streaming retries are tricky (non-idempotent).

Wei Chen (author): Updated both READMEs to reference the SDK preset names and added a dedicated “Streaming disconnects” section + guidance: do not automatically retry mid-stream unless the app is designed for it; do retry connect failures before first token; link to SDK guide.
Ibrahim Khan (review): In TS, you’re catching generic errors and retrying everything. Please gate retries to network/timeouts + 429/5xx only; do not retry 4xx other than 429.
Wei Chen (author): Updated retry.ts with status-code allowlist (429, 500, 502, 503, 504) and network errors; added note about 401/403 being config issues + link to troubleshooting.
Elliot Price (review): Please keep terminology consistent with Console: “Budgets & quotas” and “Usage dashboard (may lag ~X minutes)”. Also add a direct link placeholder that docs can redirect later.

Wei Chen (author): Copy updated to match the checklist naming; added canonical link placeholders under /docs/onboarding/production-launch and /docs/usage/usage-dashboard-latency (to be redirected by docs IA work).
Logan Wright (review): Observability section: can we make the minimal path “structured logs” first, and OTEL as optional? Also add a warning about high-cardinality labels if folks export metrics.
Wei Chen (author): Re-ordered observability.md to logs-first, OTEL optional; added note about avoiding user ids / prompts in metrics dimensions and keeping cardinality low.
Dev Patel (review): Support angle: please call out the top 3 failure modes up front (401/403, streaming disconnect, 429) and show what good logs look like for triage.
Wei Chen (author): Added “Common first-launch issues” section with example log lines and what to check in Console; also added link to support article placeholders.
release_notes: New ‘Production Readiness’ quickstart added to redwood-quickstarts.
Includes Python + TypeScript runnable examples demonstrating production-safe defaults (timeouts/retries), streaming guidance, budgets/quotas pointers, and basic observability patterns.
post_merge_followups: Add Go parity once ENG-412 helper lands everywhere (link to Go SDK reconnect helper PR).
Replace docs placeholder links with final canonical URLs after IA redirects ship.
Consider adding a minimal Dockerfile + deploy-to-Render/Fly template (out of scope for this PR).

source.title: Add optional telemetry tags/headers for template adoption metrics
source.author: Monica Patel
source.release_notes: Templates can now (optionally) tag outbound Redwood API requests with a stable, privacy-safe identifier so Console/Analytics can measure template adoption and time-to-first-success. Telemetry is opt-in and defaults to off.
source.description:
## Context
Product (PM-912) needs a reliable way to identify template-originated traffic for adoption KPIs (activation funnel, time-to-first-success, support deflection). Today, requests generated by the quickstarts are indistinguishable from hand-rolled integrations once users copy/paste code.

We want a convention that:
- Is **opt-in** (some users run behind strict corporate proxies / header allowlists)
- Is **privacy-safe** (no user identifiers, no workspace/customer IDs, no prompt content)
- Has **low-cardinality** values to avoid metrics blowups
- Works across **Python + TypeScript templates** and local dev/prod deployments
- Is visible in logs/traces for support debugging, but doesn’t require custom instrumentation

## What this PR does
Adds an optional telemetry tagging convention used by quickstart templates when calling the Redwood API:
- **Header:** `X-Redwood-Template` (string)
- **Value format:** `<template_id>@<template_version>`
  - Example: `rag-agent-python@0.3.0`
  - `template_id` is a short, static identifier for the template (no user input)
  - `template_version` is the template release version embedded at scaffold time

Also adds an optional trace/log attribute so support can correlate issues when users paste logs:
- **OTel attribute (client span):** `redwood.template` = same value as above

### Opt-in behavior
Telemetry tagging is **disabled by default**. Templates only attach `X-Redwood-Template` when `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY=1` (or `true`).

Rationale: we’ve seen corporate proxies reject unknown headers, and we don’t want templates to fail “mysteriously” on first run for users in locked-down environments.

### Redaction / privacy
- The header value is constructed exclusively from **template code constants** and the embedded template version.
- No environment variables (except the boolean enable flag) are included.
- No hostnames, org IDs, user IDs, or prompt/tool payloads are included.

## Implementation details
- Introduces a small shared helper in `packages/common/telemetry_tag.ts` and `packages/common/telemetry_tag.py` (mirrored structure across languages) that:
  - Parses `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY`
  - Produces the tag string
  - Applies it to Redwood client request options
- Stamps template version in the scaffolded app via `template.manifest.json` (already present from CLI init) and uses it as the version component.
- Updates both Python and TypeScript RAG+agent templates to plumb the tag into the Redwood client initialization.

## Example (TypeScript)
If enabled:
- Env: `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY=1`
- Outbound request includes: `X-Redwood-Template: rag-agent-ts@0.3.0`

## Example (Python)
If enabled:
- Env: `REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY=true`
- Outbound request includes: `X-Redwood-Template: rag-agent-python@0.3.0`

## Tests
- Unit tests for env parsing (accepts `1/0`, `true/false`, case-insensitive)
- Unit tests for formatting and allowed characters
- Smoke test ensures requests succeed with tagging enabled/disabled (no behavioral change on API responses)

## Notes / follow-ups
- Gateway needs to allowlist the header for hosted users. That change is tracked separately in the main `redwood` monorepo.
- If we see continued proxy issues, we can add an alternate mechanism (query param) but prefer header to avoid accidental propagation into logs at intermediaries.

## Checklist
- [x] Opt-in default off
- [x] Low-cardinality tag values
- [x] No user-controlled tag content
- [x] Documented in template README + troubleshooting
- [x] Tests added
commits: feat(common): add template telemetry tag helper and env parsing
feat(ts-template): optionally attach X-Redwood-Template header on client requests
feat(py-template): optionally attach X-Redwood-Template header on client requests
docs: describe template telemetry flag and privacy properties
test: add unit coverage for tag formatting and enablement parsing
review_conversation: Logan Wright (Analytics): Can we ensure the tag is strictly low-cardinality and not user-editable? We don’t want customers setting arbitrary values and blowing up dimensions downstream.

Monica Patel (author): Yep. The value is built from a hard-coded `TEMPLATE_ID` plus a version from the scaffold manifest. The only env var is a boolean enable flag. Added a test to ensure we never read a user-provided tag value.
Nikhil Sharma (PM): We need to measure time-to-first-success. Do we also need an event? Or is tagging enough?

Monica Patel (author): For v1 we’re doing tagging so Console can segment usage/activation metrics without introducing a new event pipeline in templates. PM-912 outlines which KPIs we can derive from tagged traffic. If we later need funnel step events, we can add them via Console SDK; keeping this scoped to request tagging for launch.

Allison Grant (Observability): Please avoid adding the tag as a span attribute on every span; only set it on the Redwood client span. Also confirm it’s not propagated into high-cardinality logs by default.

Monica Patel (author): Updated to set `redwood.template` only on the Redwood client span, not nested tool spans. For logs, we only add it to the structured request log line when debug logging is enabled (and still constant + version).
Marcus Lin (Security): Any chance this header could leak sensitive tenant info? Also ensure the README is explicit that it’s opt-in and what data is sent.
Monica Patel (author): Confirmed no tenant info. Updated README section “Telemetry tagging (optional)” to explicitly state what is and isn’t included, and that it’s disabled by default.

Brian Murphy (API/Gateway): Header name looks fine, but note that some gateways strip unknown X-* headers unless allowlisted. Make sure templates don’t fail if it’s removed and that the feature is strictly additive.
Monica Patel (author): Agree. Tagging is additive only; if stripped, requests still succeed. Also default-off to reduce the chance of first-run issues behind strict proxies.
Allison Grant (Observability): Minor naming nit: prefer `X-Redwood-Template` over `X-Template` so it’s unambiguous across vendors.
Monica Patel (author): Applied. Renamed everywhere + updated docs/tests.
Nikhil Sharma (PM): LGTM once docs mention how this shows up in Console filtering.
Monica Patel (author): Added a short note that Console filtering will land after the gateway allowlist + Console UI work (separate PRs).
Allison Grant (Observability): Approved.
Marcus Lin (Security): Approved.
Logan Wright (Analytics): Approved.
Brian Murphy (API/Gateway): Approved.
related_links: Follow-up in monorepo: gateway allowlist for X-Redwood-Template

source.title: Pin template dependencies + add vulnerability scanning workflows
source.author: Sahana Iyer
source.release_notes: Templates now pin dependencies by default and run scheduled + PR-time vulnerability scans (pip-audit/OSV for Python; npm audit/OSV for TS). Maintainers should follow docs/dependency-upgrades.md for safe upgrades and regenerating lockfiles.
source.description: Summary: This PR locks down dependency versions across the Python and TypeScript quickstart templates, adds automated vulnerability scanning in CI (PR-time + scheduled), and documents the upgrade process for template maintainers to reduce supply-chain risk. Motivation: Security review flagged unpinned transitive dependencies and lack of automated scanning (see ENG-4190 and template security review open items). We also want template repos to adhere to the Secure SDLC addendum for templates (dependency pinning, vulnerability scanning, and a repeatable upgrade story) without introducing noisy/flake-prone CI failures. Changes included: (1) Python template: move to fully pinned requirements (direct + transitive) via pip-tools, commit compiled requirements with hashes, and add a make target to regenerate; (2) TypeScript template: ensure package-lock.json is committed and consistent, add npm overrides for known vulnerable transitive packages where upstream hasn’t released yet; (3) CI: add a security scanning workflow that runs pip-audit + osv-scanner for Python and npm audit + osv-scanner for TS, with a nightly schedule and a lightweight PR check; (4) Docs: add docs/dependency-upgrades.md describing the approved upgrade cadence, how to regenerate lockfiles, how to handle false positives, and how to record remediation notes. Operational notes: The PR-time workflow is configured to avoid high-noise findings (dev-only packages in example tooling) and to fail only on high/critical CVEs by default; nightly runs are stricter and open an issue with a report artifact. Checklist: - [x] Pin Python deps with hashes - [x] Commit package-lock and align npm ci in CI - [x] Add vuln scanning workflow (PR + schedule) - [x] Document upgrade process + remediation guidelines - [x] Verify templates still install on macOS/Linux (python 3.11, node 20) - [x] No secrets in repo / no new external service tokens. Rollout: This is repo-only and should not impact Redwood API behavior. If CI noise is higher than expected, we can adjust severity thresholds or add targeted ignores with justification in docs/dependency-upgrades.md (no blanket allowlists).
commits: chore(python): compile and pin template requirements with hashes
chore(ts): commit package-lock and normalize npm ci usage
ci(security): add pip-audit + npm audit + osv-scanner workflows (PR + nightly)
docs: add dependency upgrade + remediation guide for maintainers
chore: add make/npm scripts for lockfile regeneration
conversation: Monica Patel (review): Love the direction. Two nits: (1) can we avoid pinning a pre-release for numpy/scipy equivalents if we don’t need them? (2) please ensure the template README still works with `pip install -r requirements.txt` without requiring pip-tools locally.

Sahana Iyer (author): Ack. Dropped the pre-release pin; the compiled set now stays on stable releases. README flow unchanged; pip-tools is only required for maintainers when regenerating via `make deps-update`.

Marcus Lin (review): For the scanning workflow, please avoid adding broad allowlists. If we need ignores, require a short justification + expiration date. Also: make sure scans run on forks without requiring secrets.

Sahana Iyer (author): Updated docs/dependency-upgrades.md with an ignore policy section (justification + expiry) and confirmed workflows use only GitHub-provided token permissions. Forks run PR checks without secrets; nightly issue creation is skipped on forks.
Hanae Suzuki (review): Please confirm we’re not printing env vars or token-like strings in scan output artifacts. Some tools dump environment context by default.

Sahana Iyer (author): Good catch—set `PIP_AUDIT_FORMAT=json` and upload only the JSON report; also redacted workflow step logs by default (no `set -x`). Added a note in SECURITY.md about artifact contents.
Christine Wong (review): Severity gating looks reasonable. Can we add a small note on what happens when nightly finds a vuln (who is on-call / how we triage)?

Sahana Iyer (author): Added a short triage section pointing to the DevEx rotation and linking ENG-4190 for remediation tracking; nightly workflow opens an issue with label `security` + `dependencies`.
Monica Patel (approval): Approved after latest changes. Thanks for adding the maintainers guide—this will reduce churn.
Marcus Lin (approval): Approved. Please keep the ignore mechanism strict as documented.

source.title: Add lightweight WASM edge RAG quickstart and local tool-adapter samples
source.author: Samira Patel
source.description:
Motivation: provide a compact, low-latency RAG quickstart specifically targeted at edge/web runtime deploys using WebAssembly (WASM) and lightweight local tool adapters. Many customers building on-device or edge-hosted assistants asked for a reference that demonstrates: 1) bundling a small retriever + quantized model proxy pattern that minimizes round trips to hosted inference, 2) a WASM-based tool adapter for local deterministic utilities (e.g., date normalization, currency conversion, small knowledge transforms), and 3) a progressive retrieval strategy that returns fast first-pass answers while background-updating the context.

What this PR adds (single-string description of the changeset and rationale): Adds a new quickstart under quickstarts/wasm-edge-rag that contains an end-to-end example (README + runnable scripts) showing how to: build a tiny WASM tool adapter (Rust -> wasm32-unknown-unknown) for deterministic preprocessing, spin up a minibundle that runs a lightweight retriever against a small on-device FAQ vector store (FAISS-lite bundled as a wasm module), and proxy requests to Redwood hosted inference only for heavy generation paths. Includes Node and Deno examples for running the same bundle on desktop or edge containers, plus a service-worker based demo for browsers that fall back to hosted inference when local resources are insufficient. The README has step-by-step commands, a checklist for production hardening, and guidance on cost/latency tradeoffs.

Files added/changed (high-level): quickstarts/wasm-edge-rag/README.md (new quickstart guide, architecture diagrams in ASCII), examples/wasm-tool-adapter/src/lib.rs (Rust adapter), examples/wasm-tool-adapter/wasm_build.sh (build helper), examples/wasm-tool-adapter/adapter.ts (Node wrapper for invoking wasm), examples/wasm-edge-demo/service-worker-demo/ (browser fallback demo), docs/quickstarts/wasm-edge.md (docs site entry + TOC update), tests/e2e/wasm-edge.test.ts (CI smoke), ci/workflows/quickstarts.yml (added matrix entry for node14/deno).

Testing and verification performed: local end-to-end runs on macOS/Ubuntu with Node 18 and Deno 1.35, CI matrix tested Node and Deno targets. Added e2e smoke test that verifies: build -> serve -> retriever returns nearest doc -> wasm adapter transforms tool output -> Redwood API is only called for generation stage. Performance notes included in README: median first-byte latency under 30ms on dev laptop for the local retriever path; hosted fallbacks measured ~120ms additional latency for cold-hosted calls in our test cluster.

Security and packaging notes: README includes guidance to compile WASM with --strip to reduce binary size, sign releases, and use subresource integrity for browser bundles. The wasm adapter is deterministic and runs without network privileges; sandboxing instructions are included for containerized edge deployments.

Docs and discoverability: adds docs site page (docs/quickstarts/wasm-edge.md) and updates the quickstarts index to surface this example under "Edge & Offline". The README contains a short checklist for migrating to Dedicated/Private deployments and notes on vector store persistence with encrypted blobs (customer-managed KMS).

Checklist (from PR template):
- [x] Quickstart README written and linted
- [x] Minimal CI smoke tests added
- [x] Security packaging guidance included
- [x] Examples build scripts validated on Node/Deno
- [x] Docs index updated

Backward compatibility: none (adds new examples only).

Follow-ups (tracked in ENG-9123): add a tutorial notebook for quantized local model proxies and add a Go wrapper for the wasm adapter.

node/deno wrappers and browser service-worker demo

source.title: add-rag-step-replayer-cli-quickstart
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Context: developers asked for a compact reproducible session capture and deterministic stepwise replay tool to debug RAG agents at the edge where reproducing live incidents is costly. Motivation: provide a lightweight CLI quickstart that captures tool calls, prompt inputs/outputs, and intermediate KV cache snapshots in a compressed, schema-vetted fixture format, plus a playback runner that can step through a session with assertions and telemetry hooks. This PR adds an end-to-end example, docs, tests, CI, and minimal telemetry schema. Checklist: [x] quickstart README with hands-on steps and expected outputs [x] CLI capture binary + small Go shim for portability [x] JSONL session fixture format and schema validation [x] stepwise replayer with step assertions and failure reporting [x] CI workflow that runs smoke capture+replay on push [x] sample fixtures and test harness for flaky-protection. Commits summary: "init: scaffold rag-step-replayer quickstart" adds README, CLI scaffold, and basic examples; "feat: implement capture format and basic recorder" implements session capture and compression; "feat: add replayer with step assertions and telemetry hooks" implements playback runner and assertion engine; "test: add fixtures and CI smoke tests" adds sample sessions and CI workflow; "chore: docs polish and examples" updates README and code comments. Changed areas: quickstarts/rag-step-replayer/README.md — new quickstart guide with step-by-step capture/playback instructions and troubleshooting notes; quickstarts/rag-step-replayer/cli/main.go — compact Go CLI (~420 LOC) implementing capture and playback modes with flags for output path, redact-level, and verboseness; quickstarts/rag-step-replayer/fixtures/sample_session.jsonl — sample captured session representing a 3-tool run; quickstarts/rag-step-replayer/tests/replayer_integration_test.go — integration test that runs capture->replay in CI with assertions on outputs; docs/quickstarts/rag-playbooks.md — reference entry linking the new quickstart and describing the fixture schema; .github/workflows/rag-step-replayer-ci.yml — CI workflow to build and run the integration smoke tests; telemetry/schemas/step_replay.yaml — telemetry event schema for capture/replay lifecycle. Review conversation: "Ethan Park: Nice work, this will help a lot during incident investigations. Please make the fixture size smaller and add an option to snapshot only tool calls (skip token-level KV)." "Maya Chen: Addressed — added --snapshot-mode flag (tools|full) and updated fixtures to include a compact sample. Reduced sample session to 3KB gzipped in tests." "Luis Gómez: Can we emit a minimal telemetry event for each replay step so SREs can trace failing assertions?" "Maya Chen: Added telemetry schema and optional telemetry hooks; default off for quickstart. Updated docs to show how to enable." "Sofia Patel: LGTM after small docs nit (clarify redact-level behavior)." "Maya Chen: Fixed wording and added example to README." CI summary: initial run failed lint on missing gofmt, author fixed and re-ran, subsequent run passed all checks including build, unit tests, and integration smoke test (matrix: go1.20, ubuntu-latest). Merge outcome: merged via squash after approvals from Ethan and Sofia. Notes: keeps quickstart self-contained, minimal external deps, avoids shipping production runtime code; quickstart intended as a reference for teams to adapt to Dedicated/Private deployments. How to use: install the CLI, run `replayer capture --out=session.jsonl` against a local dev agent, then `replayer replay --in=session.jsonl --assertions=examples/asserts.yaml` to step through. Support and follow-ups: recommend follow-up ENG tasks to add a Python shim for cross-language capture and a small GA telemetry dashboard (see ENG-4992).

feat: implement capture format and basic recorder
feat: add replayer with step assertions and telemetry hooks
test: add fixtures and CI smoke tests
chore: docs polish and examples
source.release_notes: Add RAG stepwise session capture and replay quickstart: provides CLI tooling, fixture format, replay assertions, and CI smoke tests to help engineers reproduce and debug RAG agent sessions.
discussion: Ethan Park: Please shrink fixture size and add tool-only snapshot mode.
Maya Chen: Added --snapshot-mode and updated fixtures to compact sample.
Luis Gómez: Request telemetry hooks per step to aid SRE debugging.
Maya Chen: Added telemetry schema and optional hooks, default disabled.
Sofia Patel: Docs nit on redact-level clarified and example added.
Maya Chen: Addressed all review comments; CI green; ready to merge.

source.title: local RAG authoring sandbox with live-reload and TypeScript tool stubs
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: onboard engineers who want a fast local iterate loop for RAG prompt development, tool-calling stubs and interactive debugging without needing a full infra stack. This quickstart provides a minimal local sandbox (browser UI + Node scaffold) with live-reload, a small in-memory vector store, example retriever adapters, and Typescript tool-stub templates that mirror Redwood tool-calling shapes. User-facing goals: (1) immediate feedback on prompt/tool interactions in the browser, (2) ability to swap in a real embedding/DB later, (3) reproducible dev loop for prompt/eval regressions. What changed: added a new quickstart at quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox that contains a web playground (React + Vite), a lightweight Node authoring server with hot-reload, a mock embedders/FAISS-like adapter, and a folder of TypeScript tool stubs for common actions (search-docs, call-rest, cite-source). The README includes a single-command start and a short guide to connect to Redwood Hosted API or local Private. Checklist: - [x] add initial quickstart files and README - [x] implement dev server with live-reload - [x] add TypeScript tool stubs and example tool-calls - [x] include small test harness and smoke tests - [x] CI lint/build passing - [x] docs site entry stub. Commits (summary): 1) add initial project scaffold and README (init commit) 2) implement Node authoring server and live-reload wiring 3) add React playground and example prompt flows 4) add TS tool-stub templates and example adapter 5) add CI smoke test, docs entry and small polish. Changed files (high level): quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/README.md; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/package.json; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/docker-compose.yml; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/authoring-server/src/index.ts; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/webapp/src/App.tsx; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/webapp/src/main.tsx; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/templates/tool-stubs.ts; quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox/test/smoke.spec.ts; docs/quickstarts/index.yml (added listing). Review conversation: "Diego: Nice dev loop, can we keep the embedders adapter interface minimal?"; "Author (Ava): pared down to a 3-method interface — embed, index, query — added comments in adapter.ts"; "Priya: docs README needs a clear security note about not using production secrets in the sandbox"; "Author: added a Security section and a one-click env.example with warnings"; "Samir: CI flaked once on the smoke test in windows runner, re-ran and passed". CI/Checks: linux-node-lint/passed, webapp-build/passed, smoke-tests/passed (rerun after flaky failure). Merge outcome: merged into main on 2024-11-12 via squash-merge. How to use (short): 1) cd quickstarts/rag-authoring-sandbox; 2) cp .env.example .env and set REDWOOD_API_KEY if you want hosted routing; 3) pnpm install && pnpm dev; 4) open http://localhost:5173 and iterate prompts, inspect tool calls, swap adapter to a real embedding provider when ready. Motivation/context links: references ENG-4251 (shortening dev iteration for prompt tuning), internal experiment notes on local dev loops. Release notes: "Add local RAG authoring sandbox with live-reload and TypeScript tool stubs for iterative prompt/tool development."

source.release_notes: Add local RAG authoring sandbox with live-reload and TypeScript tool stubs for iterative prompt/tool development. Includes a React playground, minimal in-memory retriever adapter, and example tool-call templates. Not intended for production (see SECURITY section).
source.notes_for_maintainers: This quickstart is intentionally lightweight. If we later onboard it to the docs site, prefer linking the sandbox as a dev-only example and mark it with a security badge. Follow-up work: ENG-4310 to add a real vector DB adapter examples (SQLite/Chroma) and ENG-4321 to add CI test stability improvements.

source.title: Add RAG local policy enforcer and contract-linting quickstart
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Introduces a developer quickstart demonstrating a local policy enforcer and structured-output contract linter for RAG agents. Includes examples, telemetry probes, and CI smoke tests.
source.description:
Motivation: Teams building retrieval-augmented agents often struggle to enforce tool-contract compatibility and structured-output schemas during local development. This quickstart adds a small runnable sandbox and a contract-lint that validates tool calls, JSON schemas, and policy rules before deployment. It targets developer productivity and pre-production safety checks to reduce integration bugs when moving from local authoring to Redwood-hosted or dedicated deployments.

What this PR contains: Adds a new quickstart under quickstarts/rag-policy-enforcer that includes: a) a lightweight node+python devserver that runs a stubbed RAG agent and tool adapters, b) a policy-enforcer binary (Go) that loads envelope rules (allow/deny/transform) and reports violations, c) a contract-lint CLI that checks structured-output contracts against JSON Schema and example traces, d) telemetry probe presets and a sample tracing configuration for Redwood Console, e) CI smoke runner that exercises core lint rules and sample scenarios.

Single-string checklist: - [x] Add quickstart scaffold and README with step-by-step local run instructions and expected outputs - [x] Implement contract-lint CLI (node) with JSON Schema based rules and a small rule DSL for required fields - [x] Add policy-enforcer (Go) that simulates KMS-protected rule loading and outputs granular diagnostics - [x] Add telemetry probe presets (span names, metrics, debug logs) for Console integration - [x] Add smoke CI job that runs the local devserver and ensures contract-lint passes on sample traces - [x] Update docs index for quickstarts and add cross-links to RAG troubleshooting page

Testing and verification notes: Initial CI run found two failing assertions in the contract-lint (missing example for tool:thesearch). Fixed by adding a representative example trace and expanding schema to allow null optional fields. End-to-end smoke run included 6 scenarios (happy path, missing-field, schema-mismatch, tool-error, policy-deny, transform-case) and verified enforcement and signal emission to telemetry stubs.

Rollout plan: docs-first merge. Quickstart marked experimental; will be surfaced on docs site under "RAG authoring tools" after one week of usage monitoring. Local quickstart is intentionally self-contained and opt-in to avoid surprising customers.

Related work: referenced ENG-3542 (policy DSL), PM-741 (quickstart prioritization). Cross-links in README reference the RAG authoring studio and structured-output linting docpage.

Notes for reviewers: focus on CLI ergonomics, rule DSL clarity, and telemetry signal naming. The Go policy-enforcer intentionally keeps a small dependency set to ease private deployment packaging.
commits: c1a7b2f: scaffold quickstart layout and README
f4d89ab: add contract-lint CLI and base JSON schema validators
8e3c12d: implement Go policy-enforcer and rule DSL parser
b9f7d2e: add telemetry probe presets and sample trace fixtures
d2a4b6c: CI smoke job and test scenarios; fix contract examples
review_comments: Diego Ramirez: Nice work — the rule DSL looks expressive. A few thoughts: can we simplify the error codes and make them stable for downstream parsing? Also please add a short example in README that shows a policy deny result in raw JSON.
Maya Chen (reply): Good call on stable codes; added ERR_* constants and appended a small JSON example to README-examples.md in the latest commit.
Sofia Patel: Contract-lint CLI is solid. Suggest making the default severity of missing fields 'warn' rather than 'error' to reduce churn for existing examples.
Maya Chen (reply): Changed default severity to warn and added --strict flag to fail-on-warn for CI scenarios.
Ethan Park: CI smoke job failed initially due to the missing sample for tool:thesearch; once the sample traces were added tests passed. Can we add a flaky marker to the transform-case scenario?
Maya Chen (reply): Marked transform-case as flaky and added a retry wrapper in the CI job; leaving a TODO to stabilize transform logic (ENG-3621).
Security review (automated): No high severity dependency alerts. Recommend auditing the Go binary before private packaging.
Maya Chen (reply): Added a short audit checklist and staticcheck run in the PR description and CI workflow.
Diego Ramirez: Approving after small docs tweaks.
ci_checks: smoke-quickstart: pass (3 retries for flaky scenario)
source.notes: This quickstart is marked experimental. It purposefully avoids external network dependencies and includes a small set of curated sample traces to reduce non-determinism in CI. Follow-up work: ENG-3621 (stabilize transform-case), add Rust policy-enforcer bindings (community request).
